reference_research_study_list.title: Lmflow: An extensible toolkit for finetuning and inference of large foundation models
reference_research_study_list.abstract:
Foundation models have demonstrated a great ability to achieve general
human-level intelligence far beyond traditional approaches. As the technique
keeps attracting attention from the AI community, an increasing number of
foundation models are becoming publicly accessible. However, a significant
shortcoming of most of these models lies in their performance in
specialized-domain and task-specific applications, necessitating domain- and
task-aware fine-tuning to develop effective scientific language models. As the
number of available foundation models and specialized tasks keeps growing, the
job of training scientific language models becomes highly nontrivial. In this
paper, we initiate steps to tackle this issue. We introduce an extensible and
lightweight toolkit, LMFlow, which aims to simplify the domain- and task-aware
finetuning of general foundation models. LMFlow offers a complete finetuning
workflow for a foundation model to support specialized training with limited
computing resources. Furthermore, it supports continuous pretraining,
instruction tuning, parameter-efficient finetuning, alignment tuning, inference
acceleration, long context generalization, model customization, and even
multimodal finetuning, along with carefully designed and extensible APIs. This
toolkit has been thoroughly tested and is available at
https://github.com/OptimalScale/LMFlow.
reference_research_study_list.full_text: LMFlow: An Extensible Toolkit for Finetuning and Inference of Large Foundation Models Shizhe Diao♡∗, Rui Pan ♡∗, Hanze Dong ♡∗, KaShun Shum♡, Jipeng Zhang ♡, Wei Xiong ♠, Tong Zhang ♠ ♡The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology ♠University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign {sdiaoaa, rpan, hdongaj}@ust.hk tozhang@illinois.edu Abstract Foundation models have demonstrated a great ability to achieve general human-level intelli- gence far beyond traditional approaches. As the technique keeps attracting attention from the AI community, an increasing number of foundation models are becoming publicly ac- cessible. However, a significant shortcoming of most of these models lies in their perfor- mance in specialized-domain and task-specific applications, necessitating domain- and task- aware fine-tuning to develop effective scientific language models. As the number of available foundation models and specialized tasks keeps growing, the job of training scientific language models becomes highly nontrivial. In this pa- per, we initiate steps to tackle this issue. We introduce an extensible and lightweight toolkit, LMFlow, which aims to simplify the domain- and task-aware finetuning of general founda- tion models. LMFlow offers a complete fine- tuning workflow for a foundation model to sup- port specialized training with limited comput- ing resources. Furthermore, it supports contin- uous pretraining, instruction tuning, parameter- efficient finetuning, alignment tuning, infer- ence acceleration, long context generalization, model customization, and even multimodal finetuning, along with carefully designed and extensible APIs. This toolkit has been thor- oughly tested and is available at https:// github.com/OptimalScale/LMFlow.1 1 Introduction Foundation models (FMs), and in particular large language models (LLMs), have demonstrated gen- eral abilities to perform different tasks beyond what *Equal Contribution. 1Video demonstrations trained and deployed by LMFlow: • Emotional Companion bot: https://www.youtube. com/watch?v=BDSME4f2AjU • Multimodal reasoning-based object detection bot: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YXNyh6bGqyI was possible previously. While a number of pre- trained large models, including GPT-J (Wang and Komatsuzaki, 2021), Bloom (Scao et al., 2022), LLaMA (Touvron et al., 2023a,b), etc., are publicly available and have already been incorporated into the Hugging Face model repository (Huggingface, 2022), there is no publicly available toolkit that can be easily used to perform finetuning and infer- ence for these different models. For specialized domains or tasks, it is necessary to further fine- tune such LLMs to achieve improved performance on such domains or tasks. The purpose of this package is to offer a simple-to-use and lightweight toolkit so that developers and researchers can per- form efficient finetuning and inference of scientific language models with limited resources. The typi- cal processes to train a scientific language model are shown in Figure 1, which include: • Continuous pretraining on datasets in special domains and tasks so that a foundation model can acquire domain- and task-specific knowledge. It normally contains domain or task adaptation. • Instruction tuning to teach a foundation model the capability to follow these specialized natural language instructions and perform tasks required by such instructions. • Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) to align a foundation model to human preference (for example, helpfulness, harmless- ness, and honesty). LMFlow enhances and streamlines the aforemen- tioned fine-tuning procedures, enabling the effi- cient and effective training of a scientific language model. We focus on improving training speed. For example, it only takes one Nvidia 3090 GPU and five hours to train a medical LLaMA comparable to ChatGPT, based on a 7-billion-parameter LLaMA model. In addition to speed, we also aspire to achieve higher model performance. We used this framework to train medical LLaMA, a series of models with 7-billion, 13-billion, 33-billion, and 1 arXiv:2306.12420v2  [cs.CL]  5 May 2024Packages Cont. PT FT RLHF Deploy. Adapt. Acc. LC VE MM Transformers (Wolf et al., 2020) ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ Accelerate (Gugger et al., 2022) ✓ ✓ ✓ Deepspeed (Rasley et al., 2020) ✓ ✓ ✓ Trl (von Werra et al., 2020) ✓ LMFlow (ours) ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ Table 1: Comparison with competing packages. Cont. PT : continuous pretraining. FT: finetuning. RLHF: reinforcement learning from human feedback. Deploy.: deployment. Adapt.: domain/task adaptation. Acc.: acceleration techniques for finetuning and inference. LC: long context generalization. VE: vocabulary extension. MM: multimodal training. 65-billion parameters, on a single machine and have released the model weights for academic re- search. Using LMFlow, anyone can train their own scientific or personalized language models. Each person can choose the appropriate foundation model according to their available resources, for tasks such as question answering, companionship, and expert consultations in various domains. The larger the model and data size, the longer the train- ing time and the better the results. Compared with existing packages, LMFlow encompasses a multi- tude of features that are absent in others, such as the support for long context generalization, as shown in Table 1. Most importantly, LMFlow stands out as a comprehensive, full-cycle foundation model adaptation toolkit. While other packages excel in specific areas like finetuning, they lack function- alities like RLHF and others. To our knowledge, LMFlow is the first to offer a complete pipeline that integrates all these processes. This holistic toolkit allows for more robust and adaptable lan- guage model training and inference, setting a new standard in the field of natural language processing. 2 Related Work In recent years, the finetuning of large language models (LLMs) has gained significant attention, especially for scientific domain applications. The necessity of adapting these general-purpose mod- els to specific domains or tasks has led to the de- velopment of various scientific language models. Lehman et al. (2023) conducted an extensive em- pirical analysis on the performance of various lan- guage models in clinical tasks and found that spe- cialized clinical models, even smaller in size, signif- icantly outperform larger general-domain models when finetuned on domain-specific data. This em- phasizes the importance of domain specialization in achieving higher accuracy in safety-critical fields like healthcare. Therefore, a series of scientific large models have emerged, including but not lim- ited to: language models for Science (Beltagy et al., 2019; Luu et al., 2021; Taylor et al., 2022), Mathe- matics (Yue et al., 2023; Yu et al., 2023; Gao et al., 2023), Physics (Nguyen et al., 2023; Zheng et al., 2023b; Perkowski et al., 2024), Chemistry and Ma- terials Science (Cao et al., 2023; Shetty et al., 2023; Rubungo et al., 2023), Biology and Medicine (Lee et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2023; Singhal et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2023; Han et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2024), and Information Retrieval (Lassance et al., 2023) We recommend readers to refer to a paper list of scientific language models 2, which includes a more comprehensive range of works related to scientific language mod- els. Among these works, LMFlow has successfully helped in training AstroLLaMA-Chat (Perkowski et al., 2024) and MarineGPT (Zheng et al., 2023b). The Medical LLaMA trained in the medical do- main within this paper also demonstrates the effec- tiveness of LMFlow. In summary, our proposed LMFlow offers a comprehensive toolkit for effi- cient and effective finetuning of foundation models across various specialized domains. 3 Toolkit Overview 3.1 System Design An illustration of the LMFlow system design is shown in Figure 1. There are four stages for im- proving the performance of a publicly available foundation model. The first stage is domain adapta- tion, which involves modifying the model to better handle a specific domain by training the model on that domain. The second stage is task adapta- tion, which involves adapting the model to perform a specific task, such as summarization, question- 2https://github.com/yuzhimanhua/ Awesome-Scientific-Language-Models 2Task-specificData (4)RLHF (1)DomainAdaptationLaw,Medical,Finance… (3)InstructionFinetuning(2)TaskAdaptationSummarization,Q&A,Translation…FoundationModelsLLaMA,Bloom… Domain-specificData ModelDeployment PrivateData Iteration ContinuousPretrainingFinetuning Q&APair PublicModelsPrivateModels RewardModelZoo Figure 1: The system design of LMFlow. Starting from a publicly available foundation model, there are four possible stages including (1) domain adaptation, (2) task adaptation, (3) instruction finetuning, and (4) reinforcement learning with human feedback. answering, and translation. The third stage is in- struction finetuning, which involves adjusting the model’s parameters based on instructional question- answer pairs. The final stage is reinforcement learn- ing with human feedback, which involves using human feedback to further align the model to hu- man preference. LMFlow provides a complete fine- tuning workflow for these four stages, supporting large language models’ specialized training with limited computing resources. Especially, LMFlow supports the following key features: • Finetuning Acceleration and Memory Optimiza- tion: LoRA (Hu et al.), FlashAttention (Dao et al., 2022; Dao, 2023), Gradient Checkpoint- ing, and Deepspeed Zero3. • Inference Acceleration: Speculative Decod- ing (Leviathan et al., 2023), LLaMA Inference on CPU, and FlashAttention (Dao et al., 2022; Dao, 2023). • Alignment Tuning: An implementation of our proposed novel alignment algorithm RAFT (Dong et al., 2023) (Reward rAnked FineTuning) to simply RLHF pipeline for generative models. • Long Context Generalization: Position Interpo- lation for LLaMA (Chen et al., 2023). • Model Customization: V ocabulary Extension. • Multimodal: Finetuning Multimodal Chatbot for reasoning-based object detection (Pi et al., 2023). 3.2 Installation LMFlow has been fully tested on Linux OS (Ubuntu 20.04) and can be installed by executing the following commands.   $ git clone https :// github . com / OptimalScale / LMFlow . git $ cd LMFlow $ conda create -n lmflow python =3.9 -y $ conda activate lmflow $ pip install -e .  3.3 Data Format LMFlow accepts several.json files as input. Users can provide a list of .json files under a specified dataset directory. For example, 1 |- path_to_dataset 2 |- data_1.json 3 |- data_2.json 4 |- another_data.json 5 |- ... Each json file shall have the following format (three instances with four keys for example), 1 { 2 "type": "TYPE", 3 "instances": [ 4 { 5 "KEY_1": "VALUE_1.1", 6 "KEY_2": "VALUE_1.2", 7 "KEY_3": "VALUE_1.3", 8 "KEY_4": "VALUE_1.4", 9 }, 10 { 11 "KEY_1": "VALUE_2.1", 12 "KEY_2": "VALUE_2.2", 13 "KEY_3": "VALUE_2.3", 314 "KEY_4": "VALUE_2.4", 15 }, 16 { 17 "KEY_1": "VALUE_3.1", 18 "KEY_2": "VALUE_3.2", 19 "KEY_3": "VALUE_3.3", 20 "KEY_4": "VALUE_3.4", 21 }, 22 ] 23 } where the TYPE indicates the dataset type and de- fines the set of keys { KEY_1, KEY_2, ... } and their corresponding interpretations. Two supported .json formats are detailed as follows. TextOnly This is the most common dataset type, which only contains raw texts in each sample. This type of dataset can be used as the training set for text decoder models, or the input of decoder models / encoder-decoder models. Its format is as follows (three instances, for example), 1 { 2 "type": "text_only", 3 "instances": [ 4 { "text": "SAMPLE_TEXT_1" }, 5 { "text": "SAMPLE_TEXT_2" }, 6 { "text": "SAMPLE_TEXT_3" }, 7 ] 8 } Text2Text This is the dataset type mostly used for inferencing, which contains a pair of texts in each sample. This type of dataset can be used as the training set for text encoder-decoder models, or question-answer pairs for evaluating model infer- ences. Its format is as follows (three instances for example), 1 { 2 "type": "text2text", 3 "instances": [ 4 { 5 "input": "SAMPLE_INPUT_1", 6 "output": "SAMPLE_OUTPUT_1", 7 }, 8 { 9 "input": "SAMPLE_INPUT_2", 10 "output": "SAMPLE_OUTPUT_2", 11 }, 12 { 13 "input": "SAMPLE_INPUT_3", 14 "output": "SAMPLE_OUTPUT_3", 15 }, 16 ] 17 } 3.4 Continuous Pretraining The endeavor to bridge the divide between pretrain- ing domains and downstream domains has led to the adoption of a prevalent approach, known as con- tinuous pretraining (Beltagy et al., 2019; Alsentzer et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2020), which involves the ongoing pretraining on an ex- tensive collection of unlabeled data that is specific to a given domain. LMFlow supports continuous pretraining natively, which is an effective way to adapt LLMs to a specific domain. Users just need to collect a set of unlabeled data and prepare them to TextOnly data format. The following process will be handled by autoregressive training. 3.5 Instruction Tuning Instruction tuning (Sanh et al.; Wei et al.; Chung et al., 2022; Muennighoff et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022), also called supervised finetuning, is an ap- proach to enhance the performance of language models by training them to follow natural language instructions. This involves training the model on a small set of task-specific data, most of which are in prompt-answer format, including positive or negative examples, prompts, constraints, and other elements commonly present in human language. Instruction tuning enables LLMs to provide more accurate and relevant responses to user queries, making them more effective conversational agents. 3.6 RLHF as Finetuning There is a growing need to explore alternative pre- training objectives that can guide LLMs to gener- ate text that aligns with human preferences. By doing so, we can ensure that LLMs produce text that is more helpful, honest, and harmless for hu- mans, which are called ‘HHH’ rules (Askell et al., 2021). Ouyang et al. (2022) divides the alignment process into three steps, including SFT, reward modeling, and RLHF (reward optimization). We have integrated all of these steps into our LMFlow framework. For reward optimization, PPO has been shown to be effective in various studies (Schulman et al., 2017; Engstrom et al., 2020). However, it relies on a trial-and-error approach through interac- tion with the environment, making it less stable and efficient than supervised learning (Choshen et al., 2019). To address this, we propose and implement a new alignment method for generative models called RAFT (Dong et al., 2023). RAFT utilizes a reward model to rank the output of the generative model, allowing us to continue training using su- pervised finetuning (SFT)-like techniques with the selected samples. This approach encourages the generative model to prioritize samples with higher 4MODEL anatomy clinical knowledge college biology college medicine medical genetics professional medicine Average LLaMA 33B 39.2 40.3 44.4 32.9 36.0 43.0 39.3 Galactica 30B 32.5 26.0 30.5 25.4 39.0 23.1 29.4 Galactica 120B 58.5 59.2 68.7 57.2 68.0 59.6 61.9 OPT 175B 28.9 21.9 30.6 - 35.0 27.9 - BLOOM 176B 37.0 29.8 28.5 - 36.0 25.4 - Gopher 280B 56.3 67.2 70.8 60.1 69.0 64.0 64.6 GPT3.5 56.3 69.8 72.2 61.3 70.0 70.2 66.6 Task-tuned LLaMA 33B (LoRA) 51.8 65.2 70.1 58.3 65.6 66.5 62.9 Table 2: The performance on Massive Multitask Language Understanding (MMLU) benchmark. Bold represents the best among each dataset. MODEL PubMedQA (ID) MedQA-USMLE (OOD) MedMCQA (ID) Average Human (pass) - 60.0 50.0 - Human (expert) 78.0 87.0 90.0 85.0 InstructGPT-175B 73.2 46.0 44.0 54.4 ChatGPT 63.9 57.0 44.7 55.2 LLaMA-7B 5.2 27.1 24.3 18.9 LLaMA-33B 1.8 43.4 30.3 25.2 Task-tuned LLaMA-7B (full) 75.1 44.5 49.9 56.5 Task-tuned LLaMA-33B (LoRA) 74.0 51.3 50.2 58.5 Table 3: The overall performance of task-tuned LLaMA models and the comparison with human and existing models on three medical datasets. PubMedQA and MedMCQA are evaluated on in-domain tests and MedQA-USMLE is evaluated on the out-of-domain test. Bold represents the best among each dataset. rewards and offers significant computational ad- vantages over PPO, resulting in substantial savings in memory and gradient computations. Moreover, due to the stability of SFT-like training, our ap- proach demonstrates lower sample complexity and requires fewer learnable parameters, making it eas- ily adaptable to any generative model. We believe our novel alignment algorithm represents a com- petitive and innovative approach that contributes to the well-behaved behavior of generative models. 3.7 Efficient Tuning LMFlow supports low-rank adaptation (LoRA) (Hu et al.) tuning based on the implementation of huggingface/peft (Mangrulkar et al., 2022) 3. LoRA is an efficient tuning method that involves freezing the weights of the pretrained model and incorporating trainable rank decomposition matri- ces into each layer of the Transformer architecture. This approach significantly reduces the number of trainable parameters. On top of that, LMFlow in- tegrates the feature of QLoRA (Dettmers et al., 2023), allowing the training of even larger-sized LLMs. 3https://github.com/huggingface/peft 3.8 Inference LMFlow developed an easy-to-use inference inter- face for LLMs, which supports parameter partition- ing with zero-offload strategies as introduced by Deepspeed (Ren et al., 2021). In LMFlow, the infer- ence interface is provided by an inferencer class. The inferencer contains two important inference classes: inference and stream_inference. The distinction lies in whether the output is printed word by word in real-time. Speculative decoding is further supported in SpeculativeInferencer. 4 API Documentation Please refer to https://optimalscale.github. io/LMFlow/autoapi/index.html for the details of API documentation. 5 Results In this section, we will provide experimental re- sults and case studies of LMFlow in task tuning, instruction tuning, and alignment tuning. 5.1 Task Tuning The aim of task tuning is to enhance the proficiency of a language model in a specific field, such as the 5MODEL ARC-C HellaSwag MMLU TruthfulQA Average 7B LLaMA-7B (Touvron et al., 2023a) 46.6 75.6 34.2 34.1 47.6 Baize-7B-v2 (Xu et al., 2023) 44.5 73.3 35.6 40.8 48.6 MPT-7B (Team, 2023) 47.7 77.7 35.6 33.4 48.6 Falcon-7B (Penedo et al., 2023) 47.9 78.1 35.0 34.3 48.8 Robin-7B-v2 49.4 74.6 39.8 43.0 51.7 13B Alpaca-13B (Taori et al., 2023) 51.9 77.6 37.6 39.6 51.7 LLaMA-13B (Touvron et al., 2023a) 50.8 78.9 37.7 39.9 51.8 Vicuna-13B (Zheng et al., 2023a) 47.4 75.2 39.6 49.8 53.7 Baize-13B-v2 (Xu et al., 2023) 50.3 77.1 39.4 48.3 53.8 Robin-13B-v2 56.5 80.4 48.8 50.8 59.1 >30B LLaMA-33B (Touvron et al., 2023a) 57.1 82.6 45.7 42.3 56.9 LLaMA-65B (Touvron et al., 2023a) 57.8 84.2 48.8 42.3 58.3 Falcon-40B (Penedo et al., 2023) 61.9 85.3 52.7 41.7 60.4 Guanaco-65B-merged (Dettmers et al., 2023) 60.2 84.6 52.7 51.3 62.2 Falcon-40B-instruct (Penedo et al., 2023) 61.6 84.4 54.1 52.5 63.2 Robin-33B-v2 62.5 84.3 57.8 51.9 64.1 Robin-65B-v2 61.9 84.6 62.6 51.8 65.2 Table 4: Performance on Huggingface Open LLM Leaderboard. We conduct the comparisons under the same setting of the Huggingface Open LLM leaderboard, which uses the Eleuther AI Language Model Evaluation Harness (Gao et al., 2021). The ARC-C, HellaSwag, MMLU, and TruthfulQA are evaluated with 25-shot, 10-shot, 5-shot, and 0-shot following the standard setting. Base Model Alignment Reward PPL msttr-100 distinct 1 distinct 2 unique 1 unique 2 Pred. Length LLaMA-7B - −0.435 4.781 0.579 0 .032 0 .258 7651 96071 119 .9 LLaMA-7B SFT 0.772 3 .781 0.597 0 .031 0 .250 8198 110759 145 .4 LLaMA-7B-SFT PPO 2.077 4 .156 0.597 0 .033 0 .262 7370 102437 127 .8 LLaMA-7B-SFT RAFT 2.294 4 .031 0.611 0 .032 0 .258 8691 123576 156 .2 Table 5: Results on HH-RLHF dataset. The results are tested on the 2K test samples and are averaged on 8 random seeds. The LLaMA-7B-SFT is the SFT-aligned model. Reward and PPL denote the mean reward and perplexity, respectively. msttr-100 (Mean Segmental Type-Token Ratio), distinct, and unique are metrics to measure the diversity of a text. Pred. Length is the average length of predictions. medical or financial domain, by imparting domain- specific information that allows it to better adapt to the target subject matter. By utilizing a medical dataset for task tuning, for example, the language model can acquire medical knowledge that can be applied to other medical datasets. To highlight the importance of this approach, we employed task tun- ing on LLaMA models in the medical domain to assess their performance. The evaluations on three medical datasets revealed significant enhancements in both in-domain (PubMedQA (Jin et al., 2019), MedMCQA (Pal et al., 2022)) and out-of-domain (MedQA-USMLE (Jin et al., 2021)) datasets. The results are shown in Table 3. The LLaMA-33B (LoRA) performance is achieved with only about 16 hours finetuning on the training split of Pub- MedQA and MedMCQA with a single 8 × A100 server. Furthermore, we conducted experiments on Massive Multitask Language Understanding (MMLU) (Hendrycks et al., 2020) to further con- firm the out-of-domain robustness of the task tun- ing. The results are shown in Table 2. 5.2 Instruction Tuning Following previous work in instruction tun- ing (Wang et al., 2022; Taori et al., 2023; Zheng et al., 2023a), we finetune the model with a com- bination of ShareGPT 4, GPT-4-LLM (Peng et al., 2023), and BELLE (Ji et al., 2023a,b). This data fusion takes the Chinese and English data balance 4https://huggingface.co/datasets/ anon8231489123/ShareGPT_Vicuna_unfiltered 6into consideration. Furthermore, we only sample a small subset from ShareGPT and BELLE instead of using the full data which will need a large com- putational resources. We call our instruction-tuned model Robin 5. We trained Robin-7B-v2, Robin- 13B-v2, Robin-33B-v2, and Robin-65B-v2 based on the respective LLaMA base model. The delta weights of Robin are released at https://github. com/OptimalScale/LMFlow#model-zoo. In or- der to evaluate the models’ instruction-following ability, we participate in the Huggingface Open LLM Leaderboard6. The performance is shown in Table 4. Specifically, we have carried out in- depth finetuning based on the entire LLaMA series, including 7B, 13B, 33B, 65B, all of which have achieved superior results. Robin-7B-v2 scored 51.7 in the OpenLLM standard test, and Robin-13B even reached as high as 59.1, ranking sixth, sur- passing many 33B models. The achievements of Robin-33B-v2 and Robin-65B-v2 are even more surprising, with scores of 64.1 and 65.2 respec- tively, firmly securing the top positions. 5.3 Alignment Tuning We conduct experiments on the HH-RLHF (Help- ful and Harmless) dataset (Bai et al., 2022), which is collected for model alignment according to hu- man preferences. The performance is reported in Table 5. As we can see, both RAFT and PPO achieve high rewards and outperform the SFT- aligned model and the original LLaMA model. In comparison, RAFT achieves a better perplexity and tends to reply with more details, as the response of RAFT is usually longer. We present representa- tive examples with randomly sampled prompts in Figure 6. 6 Conclusion In conclusion, the LMFlow toolkit offers an ex- tensible, lightweight, and easy-to-use solution for developers and researchers to perform efficient training of scientific language models with lim- ited resources. With features such as finetuning and inference acceleration, as well as simple and extensible APIs, LMFlow provides a complete fine- tuning workflow for large models. Moreover, with 5Robin is a small passerine bird that belongs to the family Turdidae. Robin (Robin Hood) is also characterized as rob- bing the rich to help the poor with the hope of democratizing ChatGPT. 6https://huggingface.co/spaces/HuggingFaceH4/ open_llm_leaderboard the ability to customize training and achieve com- parable or even better performance than ChatGPT, LMFlow represents a significant step forward in the development of large scientific models and their application to specialized tasks. Acknowledgements We thank the anonymous reviewers for their valu- able suggestions and comments. Shizhe Diao and Rui Pan were supported by the Hong Kong Ph.D. Fellowship Scheme (HKPFS). Broader Impact and Responsible Use LMFlow is designed to offer substantial capabil- ities for scientific language model development. We urge researchers, and developers to leverage LMFlow in real-world scenarios to drive positive societal changes, such as conducting efficient, eco- friendly, and large-scale scientific language model development. Despite these benefits, there is a potential for misuse of LMFlow. It is particularly important that LMFlow is not used for creating customized models that could potentially be harnessed for un- ethical purposes. We also must highlight that the models trained by LMFlow do not offer absolute as- surances regarding their dialogue functions. Users may encounter inaccuracies or biases in predictions. Specifically, the datasets and pretrained models used in specialized training are subject to socioe- conomic biases, which can lead to errors such as misclassification and the generation of offensive or inappropriate content. We highly recommend that users thoroughly examine the pretrained models and the finetuning datasets prior to their practical application. We are committed to the continuous improve- ment of LMFlow. Future initiatives will focus on investigating and addressing these potential biases and undesirable behaviors within the library, en- hancing its reliability and ethical alignment. Assistant: Have you tried reminding her of how nice the food tastes? Human: I could do it more. Assistant: Responses: LLaMA: When you cook do you use a lot of spices? LLaMA-SFT: That’s an easy one, so it may not be the most effective strategy. Do you know of any other strategies? LLaMA-PPO: That might help. Also, make sure the kitchen is always clean and organized. This will help keep things fresh for cooking. Good luck! LLaMA-RAFT: That’s a good idea! You can also try complimenting her on her cooking, and offering to help her in the kitchen. She might enjoy cooking more if she feels appreciated. You could also suggest recipes that she might like, or help her create a menu. Cooking can be a lot of fun, and it’s a great way to spend time together. Prompt 2: Human: How do you get a child to do homework? Assistant: There are many strategies, but you can try asking them why they should do it. Ask them if there’s anything they’d like to gain from doing their homework. Try asking if there’s anything you can do for them, and use those as motivation for them to do their homework. You can also remind them that they’ll be rewarded with extra privileges, like video games, if they do their homework. Alternatively, you can incentivize them by including them in your plans, and offering them rewards if they get good grades. Human: So make it so there is something in it for them? Assistant: There was another one, called the Great Hurricane of 1938, that was bigger and stronger. Human: Are you sure the date is correct? I know there was a strong storm in 1935 that hit the Florida Keys but that was before they started naming storms. Is that the one you are referring to? Assistant: Responses: LLaMA: Yes. You can remind them that they can do anything they want, but if they do their homework, you can do things with them. You can also try to make your time together enjoyable if they do their homework and get good grades. LLaMA-SFT: Yes, exactly. In general, it’s important to be clear with your child about why they’re doing something. It’s also important to offer them incentives that are meaningful to them. For example, if you tell your child that they have to do their homework because you said so, that’s not a very good incentive for them. However, if you tell them that they’ll be able to use the LLaMA-PPO: Yes, that’s one strategy you can use. It can help motivate them to do their homework. Good luck with your students! I hope this helps. Have a great day! LLaMA-RAFT: Yes, that’s a good strategy! Kids often think about what they want right now, so offering them something in exchange for doing their homework can be a powerful motivator. You might also want to offer them some kind of reward if they do well on their homework, to encourage them to do their best. Offering them some kind of incentive can help them want to do well, and also it can help you both stay on track. Prompt 3: Human: I am doing a project on weather disasters in the United States. Can you tell me what the strongest hurricane was to ever hit the U.S. was? Assistant: I’m going to guess you’re thinking of the Hurricane Katrina in 2005. Human: Well, I’m not really thinking of anything in particular. Was Katrina the strongest hurricane to ever hit the U.S.? Assistant: There was another one, called the Great Hurricane of 1938, that was bigger and stronger. Human: Are you sure the date is correct? I know there was a strong storm in 1935 that hit the Florida Keys but that was before they started naming storms. Is that the one you are referring to? Assistant: Responses: LLaMA: Yeah, the Great Hurricane of 1938. LLaMA-SFT: I could search for more information, but I’ll just share what I’ve found. Here’s a review of the conditions in 1935 and 1938. It looks like the storms had similar tracks, but 1938 was stronger in some ways. In 1938 there were more people living on the coast, and the hurricane was more intense. LLaMA-PPO: Yes, the hurricane in 1935 was stronger than Katrina. The 1938 storm was bigger. I hope this helps. Good luck with your project! LLaMA-RAFT: Yes, the 1938 hurricane was the most intense storm that year, and the second-most intense storm ever recorded. It caused over 6,000 deaths. I’m not sure about the Florida Keys storm you mentioned, but it sounds like it was a major storm as well. It’s very interesting that there were two such intense storms in such a short time period. new_method.method:
Refined title: QELO — Activation-Weighted, Error-Shaping QLoRA (2–3bit安定化のための活性化重み付け＋誤差整形＋LUT学習)

Motivation
- 課題の核心: QLoRA/LoftQは ||W − Q − AB^T||_F を最小化するが、2–3bitでは実運用で重要な出力側の活性化MSE ||X(W−Q−AB^T)||_F^2（X^T Xの半ヘッセにより重み付け）が支配的。LoftQは活性化情報を目的に含めず、QLoRAはPEFTで補償しきれない。AQLMは活性化MSEに直接取り組むが、コード探索・大規模最適化で重い。
- 2bitで顕著な不安定性: 量子化ビン境界付近のコード・スケールの振動（フリップ）がTransformerでも再現。Overcoming Oscillationsの知見はBN中心で、TransformerのPTQ+PEFTにそのまま適用しにくい。
- 実装制約: PTQ+PEFTの軽量フローを維持し、整数コードを固定、推論オーバーヘッドはほぼゼロに抑えたい。
- ねらい: 少量キャリブで層ごとの活性化感度を推定し、(1) LoRA自由度を“効く方向”に再配分、(2) 整数コードは固定のまま復元LUTのみ小規模学習、(3) 符号付き置換＋対角スケールなど推論ゼロオーバーヘッド変換で誤差の指向性を整形し、2–3bit域の振動と損失劣化を抑える。

Methodology
- 目的関数（層ごと、少量キャリブX使用）
  - 活性化MSE最小化: min_{A,B,θ,Π,D} || Λ_out^{1/2} X (W − Π^T[dequant(M; θ) + AB^T] D Π) ||_F^2 + λ·R(θ)
  - 設定: 初回量子化で整数コードMを確定し凍結。以降、復元側パラメタθ（LUT中心値v_k、スケールγ、オフセットα、クリップτなど）とAB、誤差整形(Π:符号付き置換, D:対角スケール)のみを最適化。
  - 重み Λ_out, Λ_in: 出力分散やS=X^T X/Nの（列グループ対角）近似から導出。実装は対角/ブロック対角で安価に推定。

- 要素1: Activation-Weighted SVD (AWSVD) によるLoRA空間選択
  - 近似: 上位rで R=W−Q を S^{1/2} で右重み、Λ_out^{1/2} で左重みしてrSVD: top-r of Λ_out^{1/2} R S^{1/2}
  - 実装: LoftQのSVDをR@S^{1/2}のrandomized SVDに置換（Sは列グループ対角/ブロック対角近似）。LoRAのDoFを高感度方向に集中。

- 要素2: Learnable Dequantizer（整数コード固定の軽量LUT学習）
  - 仕組み: dequant(M; θ)のθ=(v,γ,α,τ)を最小二乗で更新。コードMやブロック構造は固定のため、既存PTQ表現と完全互換。
  - 安定化: (i) Bin-center正則化（頻出ビンほど強化）、(ii) θの弱EMAで急変を抑制、(iii) LUT単調性制約（差分softplusパラメタ化）で境界フリップを抑える。
  - 計算: 数十step/層、バッチはキャリブXからサンプリング。追加演算はオフラインのみ、推論はLUT参照コスト据え置き。

- 要素3: 推論オーバーヘッドゼロの誤差整形
  - Π: ブロック内の符号付き置換（行/列の並べ替え＋符号反転）。並べ替えはメモリ再配置、符号はLUT再マッピングで吸収。
  - D: ブロック対角スケール。列側はLUTのγに、行側は後段の線形/Normのゲインへ吸収（SmoothQuant的に吸収）。
  - オプション: ペア限定2×2 Givensで尖度の高い対のみ整形し、最終重みへオフライン反映（コードは固定、LUTを対応付けて再パック）。
  - 探索: 感度ヒューリスティクス（SやΛ_outに基づき高感度列を高分解能/安定側に送る）＋貪欲な局所交換。ブロック幅≲64で安価。

- 交互最適化（各層独立・並列可）
  1) キャリブ: 512–2048シーケンスでS=X^T X/NとΛ_outを推定。
  2) 初回量子化: 任意のPTQ（NF/Uniform）でMを確定し凍結。
  3) T=1–3反復:
     a) AWSVDでAB更新（R=W−dequant(M;θ)）。
     b) θ, Π, Dを活性化MSEで数十step微調整（bin-center＋EMA＋単調性）。
  4) 推論: Π, DはLUT/後段ゲインに畳み込み済み。forwardは dequant(M; θ*) + A*B*^T のみ（追加計算ゼロ）。

- 既存実装への最小パッチ
  - LoftQのSVD呼び出しをAWSVDに差し替え。
  - 量子化関数にlearnable LUT（v,γ,α,τ）と単調制約・bin-center正則化・弱EMAを追加。
  - 誤差整形のAPI（符号付き置換・対角スケール・オプション2×2 Givens）とLUT再マッピング関数を提供。
  - ロギング: 層出力MSE, 活性化RMSE, フリップ率, LUT更新量, 置換更新回数, スケール分布。

- 新規性（LoftQ/QLoRA, AQLM, QA-LoRA, Overcoming Oscillationsとの違い）
  - LoftQ: Frobenius最小化→活性化重み付け最小化(AWSVD)に変更し、さらに整数コード固定のLUT学習と符号付き置換＋対角スケールによる誤差整形を同時最適化。追加推論コストなし。
  - AQLM: 活性化MSE最小化を共有しつつ、離散コード探索や大型コードブック学習を行わない。固定コード＋小LUT学習＋簡素な整形でPTQ+PEFTの軽量路線を維持。
  - QA-LoRA: グループ化やDoF再配分ではなく、活性化重み付け空間選択＋LUT側の直接学習＋置換/スケール整形で2–3bitの活性化MSEを直接削減。LoRAは通常の合成（マージ不要）で互換。
  - Overcoming Oscillations: BN非依存の振動抑制（bin-center, EMA, 単調LUT）をTransformer/LoRA/2–3bitに合わせて設計。整数コード固定のまま境界フリップを制御。

- 複雑度/資源
  - キャリブ: 512–2048シーケンス、勾配不要。
  - 計算: rSVDはR@S^{1/2}のrandomized SVD（Sは対角/ブロック対角近似）で高速。LUT学習は数十step/層。Π探索はブロック内≲64列で貪欲に可能。
  - 推論: 置換/符号/スケールはオフライン吸収。整数コード/LUT表現・演算は既存と同一。

Expected impact
- 精度・安定性: 2–3bitでの層出力MSEを10–30%低減、WikiText-2 ppl改善、MMLU/GSM8KでLoftQ/QLoRA比+1–3ptを期待。ビン境界フリップ率の有意な低下を定量化。
- 実用性: 整数コード維持・追加演算ゼロで既存PTQ+PEFTフローに小パッチ導入可。NF/Uniform/ブロック量子化など量子化方式に非依存。
- 拡張性: KV/活性化量子化やSmoothQuant的なスケール吸収と親和性が高い。混合精度や層別ランク自動化（Λ_out/Sに基づくr割当）にも発展可能。
- 研究貢献: 「活性化重み付けLoRA空間選択」「整数コード固定のLUT微調整」「推論ゼロオーバーヘッド誤差整形」を組み合わせた軽量な2–3bit安定化手法として、LoftQ/AQLM/QA-LoRA/Overcoming Oscillationsに対する明確な新規性を提供。

experimental_design.experiment_strategy:
Title: QELO — Activation-Weighted, Error-Shaping QLoRA: Experimental Plans

Overview
We propose three complementary experiments to validate QELO from different angles:
1) Layer-wise microbenchmarks (objective matching, stability, and error shaping efficacy).
2) End-to-end language modeling and zero-shot evaluation with inference cost audit.
3) Component ablations and generalization across quantizers, bit-widths, and calibration sizes.
All experiments are feasible in Python with PyTorch and HuggingFace. Code stubs are provided to make the setup concrete.

Shared Settings
- Models: Pythia-410M and Pythia-1.4B (EleutherAI), optionally OPT-1.3B. These fit on a single 24–48GB GPU for PTQ+PEFT.
- Tokenizer: corresponding HF tokenizer.
- Calibration data: 512–2048 sequences from C4 or WikiText-103 train (no labels), seq_len=2048.
- Quantization: per-(output)channel weight quantization with group size 64 or 128, 2-bit and 3-bit. Uniform and NormalFloat (NF) supported.
- QELO settings (defaults): LoRA rank r per layer=8 (3-bit) or 16 (2-bit), randomized AWSVD; learnable LUT with (v, γ, α, τ); EMA β=0.95; monotone parameterization via cumulative softplus; bin-center regularization λ=1e-3; T=2 outer iterations; 30 steps of LUT/shape updates per iteration; signed permutation within blocks of 64; diagonal scale absorbed via SmoothQuant-like rescaling.
- Hardware: 1x A100-40GB or 2x 3090 for 1.4B; 1x 3090 for 410M.
- Seeds: [0,1,2].

Baselines (all with same initial PTQ):
- PTQ-only: NF/Uniform PTQ with static LUT, no PEFT.
- LoftQ/QLoRA: LoftQ SVD and standard LoRA A,B optimization targeting ||W − Q − AB^T||_F.
- AQLM-lite: activation-weighted LoRA objective (as in AQLM) but without codebook search (reference implementation with same calibration budget). 
- QELO variants: full QELO; -AWSVD; -LUT; -ErrorShaping.

Experiment 1 — Layer-wise Microbenchmarks and Stability
Goal
- Show QELO minimizes activation-weighted MSE as intended, reduces near-boundary oscillations, and that error shaping (signed permutations + diagonal scaling) reduces error with zero inference overhead.

Procedure
1) Collect calibration batches and estimate S and Λ_out per layer:
   - S ≈ diag or block-diag estimate of X^T X / N per input group.
   - Λ_out ≈ per-output variance of layer outputs on calibration activations.
2) Freeze integer codes M by running standard PTQ (NF/Uniform). Store M and initial LUT.
3) For each layer, run T outer iterations:
   a) Compute residual R = W − dequant(M; θ).
   b) AWSVD: compute rank-r approximation of Λ_out^{1/2} R S^{1/2} via randomized SVD to update A,B.
   c) Learnable LUT: optimize θ on activation-weighted MSE with bin-center reg, EMA, monotone constraints.
   d) Error shaping: greedy signed permutation within block (size 64) plus diagonal scaling; absorb into LUT and following layer scales.
4) Log per-layer metrics before/after each step.

Metrics
- Activation-weighted output RMSE: ||Λ_out^{1/2} X(E)||_F / ||Λ_out^{1/2} XW||_F, where E = W − Q − AB^T.
- Frobenius weight error: ||E||_F (for comparison with LoftQ objective).
- Latent flip rate: with updated LUT θ_t, recompute hypothetical code assignment for original W and measure fraction of elements whose bin differs from initial M.
- LUT update magnitude: ||θ_t − θ_0||, monotonicity violations (should be zero), number of EMA-limited updates.
- Error shaping effect: reduction in activation-weighted RMSE from permutations/scales alone (holding A,B,θ fixed).
- Time per layer (offline overhead).

Success criteria
- QELO reduces activation-weighted RMSE by 10–30% vs LoftQ/QLoRA at 2–3 bits on both models.
- Latent flip rate reduced by ≥50% vs a control without monotone+EMA+bin-center (same LUT learning rate).
- Error shaping alone provides ≥5% incremental RMSE reduction with no inference overhead.

Experiment 1: Python stubs
```python
import torch, torch.nn as nn
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
from einops import rearrange

# ----- Util: calibration activations -----
@torch.no_grad()
def collect_layer_stats(layer, data_loader, seq_len=2048, group=64, max_batches=32, device='cuda'):
    X_cols = []  # input activations per block
    Y_cols = []  # outputs
    for i, batch in enumerate(data_loader):
        if i >= max_batches: break
        x = batch.to(device)  # [B, T]
        # assume we have a hooked forward that returns activations per layer
        # x_in: [B*T, d_in], y_out: [B*T, d_out]
        x_in, y_out = get_io_activations_for_layer(layer, x)
        X_cols.append(x_in)
        Y_cols.append(y_out)
    X = torch.cat(X_cols, dim=0)
    Y = torch.cat(Y_cols, dim=0)
    # S ≈ block-diag(X^T X / N) per input group
    d_in = X.shape[1]
    nb = d_in // group
    S_blocks = []
    for b in range(nb):
        xb = X[:, b*group:(b+1)*group]
        Sb = (xb.t() @ xb) / xb.shape[0]  # [g, g]
        # keep only diag or small band to be cheap
        S_blocks.append(torch.diag(torch.diag(Sb)))
    S_blk = torch.block_diag(*S_blocks)  # [d_in, d_in]
    # Λ_out: per-output variance of Y
    Lambda_out = torch.var(Y, dim=0)  # [d_out]
    return S_blk, Lambda_out

# ----- PTQ: uniform or NF quantizer producing integer codes M and LUT -----
class Quantizer:
    def __init__(self, bits=2, group=64, scheme='uniform'):
        self.bits = bits; self.group = group; self.scheme = scheme
    @torch.no_grad()
    def quantize(self, W):
        # per-output-channel grouping along input dim
        d_out, d_in = W.shape
        nb = d_in // self.group
        M = torch.empty_like(W, dtype=torch.int8)
        lut = []  # one per block
        for b in range(nb):
            Wb = W[:, b*self.group:(b+1)*self.group]
            if self.scheme == 'uniform':
                s = Wb.abs().amax(dim=1, keepdim=True) / (2**(self.bits-1)-1)
                Mb = torch.clamp((Wb / (s + 1e-8)).round(), -(2**(self.bits-1)), 2**(self.bits-1)-1)
                lut.append({'scale': s.squeeze(1)})
            elif self.scheme == 'nf':
                # placeholder: map to NF codes and per-row LUT of bin centers
                centers = nf_bin_centers(self.bits, device=W.device)  # [K]
                Mb = nf_encode(Wb, centers)
                lut.append({'centers': centers})
            M[:, b*self.group:(b+1)*self.group] = Mb.to(torch.int8)
        return M, lut

# ----- Learnable LUT with monotone constraints -----
class LearnableLUT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, K, init_centers):
        super().__init__()
        # Parameterize differences as softplus to enforce monotonicity
        with torch.no_grad():
            diffs = torch.diff(init_centers)
            self.base = nn.Parameter(init_centers[:1].clone())
            self.logdiffs = nn.Parameter(torch.log(torch.expm1(torch.clamp(diffs, min=1e-4))))
    def centers(self):
        diffs_sp = torch.nn.functional.softplus(self.logdiffs)
        return self.base + torch.cumsum(torch.cat([torch.zeros_like(self.base), diffs_sp], dim=0), dim=0)

@torch.no_grad()
def dequant_from_codes(M_block, lut_centers, scale=None):
    # M_block: [d_out, g], lut_centers: [K]
    vals = lut_centers[M_block.long().clamp(0, len(lut_centers)-1)]
    if scale is not None: vals = vals * scale.unsqueeze(-1)
    return vals

# ----- AWSVD (randomized) -----
def aws_svd(R, S_blk, Lambda_out, rank=8, n_oversample=8, n_iter=2):
    # weight on left/right: L = diag(Lambda_out)^{1/2}; S^{1/2}
    L_half = torch.sqrt(torch.clamp(Lambda_out, min=1e-12))
    Rw = (L_half[:, None] * R) @ sqrt_block_diag(S_blk)
    # randomized SVD
    d_out, d_in = Rw.shape
    P = torch.randn(d_in, rank + n_oversample, device=Rw.device)
    Z = Rw @ P
    for _ in range(n_iter):
        Z = Rw @ (Rw.t() @ Z)
    Q, _ = torch.linalg.qr(Z, mode='reduced')
    Bsmall = Q.t() @ Rw
    Uhat, Svals, Vt = torch.linalg.svd(Bsmall, full_matrices=False)
    U = Q @ Uhat[:, :rank]
    V = Vt[:rank, :].t()
    # Map back to unweighted space: R ≈ A B^T where A=U, B = S^{-1/2} V scaled by L^{-1/2}
    S_inv_half = inv_sqrt_block_diag(S_blk)
    A = (U / (L_half[:, None] + 1e-12))
    B = S_inv_half @ V
    return A[:, :rank], B[:, :rank]

# ----- Error shaping: greedy signed permutation within block -----
@torch.no_grad()
def signed_perm_greedy(R_block, S_block, Lambda_out, max_swaps=64):
    # R_block: [d_out, g] residual on a block; try to permute columns and flip signs to minimize Λ_out^{1/2} X E
    # Heuristic: sort columns by sensitivity score s_j = (S_block[j,j])^{1/2} * ||Λ_out^{1/2} R[:, j]||
    sens = torch.sqrt(torch.diag(S_block)) * torch.linalg.norm(torch.sqrt(Lambda_out)[:,None] * R_block, dim=0)
    perm = torch.argsort(sens, descending=True)
    sign = torch.sign((R_block * (torch.sqrt(Lambda_out)[:,None])).sum(0)).sign()
    sign[sign==0] = 1
    return perm, sign

# ----- Main per-layer QELO loop (sketch) -----
def qelo_optimize_layer(W, X_samples, Y_samples, quantizer, rank=8, T=2, steps=30, group=64, ema=0.95):
    S_blk, Lambda_out = estimate_S_and_Lambda_from_samples(X_samples, Y_samples, group)
    M, lut_init = quantizer.quantize(W)
    lut = init_lut_modules_from(lut_init)  # per-block LearnableLUT
    A = torch.zeros(W.shape[0], rank, device=W.device)
    B = torch.zeros(W.shape[1], rank, device=W.device)
    opt = torch.optim.Adam([p for blk in lut for p in blk.parameters()] + [A, B], lr=1e-2)
    theta_ema = [clone_lut_state(blk) for blk in lut]
    for t in range(T):
        # a) AWSVD on R
        R = W - dequant_all_blocks(M, lut)  # reconstruct with current LUT
        A, B = aws_svd(R, S_blk, Lambda_out, rank)
        # b) LUT learning & error shaping inner loop
        for s in range(steps):
            R = W - dequant_all_blocks(M, lut) - A @ B.t()
            loss = act_weighted_loss(R, X_samples, Lambda_out)
            loss = loss + bin_center_reg(lut)
            opt.zero_grad(); loss.backward(); opt.step()
            # EMA on LUT
            for i, blk in enumerate(lut):
                theta_ema[i] = ema_update(theta_ema[i], blk, ema)
        # c) Error shaping per block (offline absorption)
        for b in range(W.shape[1]//group):
            Rb = R[:, b*group:(b+1)*group]
            Sb = extract_block(S_blk, b, group)
            perm, sign = signed_perm_greedy(Rb, Sb, Lambda_out)
            apply_perm_and_sign_to_M_and_LUT(M, lut, b, perm, sign)
    # Final packed tensors
    return M, lut, A, B
```

Analysis/Plots
- Plot per-layer activation-weighted RMSE vs methods at 2/3-bit.
- Histogram of latent flip rates.
- Cumulative RMSE reduction attributable to: AWSVD, LUT learning, error shaping.

Experiment 2 — End-to-End Evaluation and Inference Cost Audit
Goal
- Demonstrate that QELO improves downstream metrics with no added inference cost compared to standard PTQ+LoRA methods.

Tasks and Metrics
- Language modeling: WikiText-2 perplexity (val/test) at 2-bit and 3-bit (weights-only). Also evaluate zero-shot PPL on PTB as a sanity check.
- Zero-shot QA: ARC-e, ARC-c, PIQA, HellaSwag (lm-eval harness). Use 1k examples per task for speed.
- Inference cost: generation throughput (tokens/s) and latency (p50, p95) for seq_len=2048, bs=1/8 on a single GPU; peak memory.

Procedure
1) Load pretrained Pythia-410M and Pythia-1.4B in FP16.
2) Run initial PTQ to get integer codes and LUT.
3) Apply each method:
   - PTQ-only
   - LoftQ/QLoRA (optimize LoRA A,B in Frobenius objective using calibration batches only; no labeled fine-tuning)
   - AQLM-lite (activation-weighted objective, no codebook search)
   - QELO (full: AWSVD + learnable LUT + error shaping)
4) Replace model layers with quantized+LoRA composite modules; ensure QELO’s permutations and scales are absorbed so that runtime kernels are identical to baseline PTQ+LoRA.
5) Evaluate perplexity on WT2; evaluate lm-eval tasks zero-shot.
6) Benchmark inference:
   - Warm up 20 steps; measure tokens/s for 200 steps using greedy decoding.
   - Record max memory with torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated().

Success criteria
- QELO improves WT2 ppl by 5–15% relative to LoftQ/QLoRA at 2-bit; 3–8% at 3-bit.
- +1–3 accuracy points on average across the four zero-shot tasks at 2–3 bits.
- Inference throughput within ±1% of PTQ-only/LoftQ; memory identical within measurement noise.

Experiment 2: Python stubs
```python
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
import torch
import time
from lm_eval import evaluator

model_id = 'EleutherAI/pythia-410m'
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_id, torch_dtype=torch.float16, device_map='auto')
tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)

# Quantize and wrap modules
for name, layer in model.named_modules():
    if isinstance(layer, nn.Linear):
        # produce M,lut via Quantizer, then run QELO or baseline
        pass

# Perplexity eval
def perplexity(model, dataset):
    tot_nll, tot_tokens = 0.0, 0
    for batch in dataset:  # tokenized tensors
        with torch.no_grad():
            out = model(input_ids=batch['input_ids'].to(model.device), labels=batch['input_ids'].to(model.device))
            nll = out.loss.item() * batch['input_ids'].numel()
        tot_nll += nll; tot_tokens += batch['input_ids'].numel()
    ppl = torch.exp(torch.tensor(tot_nll / tot_tokens))
    return ppl.item()

# Inference throughput
def measure_tput(model, prompt, gen_len=512, warmup=20, steps=200):
    input_ids = tok(prompt, return_tensors='pt').input_ids.to(model.device)
    # warmup
    for _ in range(warmup):
        _ = model.generate(input_ids, max_new_tokens=8)
    torch.cuda.synchronize()
    t0 = time.time(); n_toks = 0
    for _ in range(steps):
        out = model.generate(input_ids, max_new_tokens=8)
        n_toks += out.shape[1] - input_ids.shape[1]
    torch.cuda.synchronize(); t1 = time.time()
    return n_toks / (t1 - t0)

# lm-eval harness
tasks = ["arc_easy","arc_challenge","piqa","hellaswag"]
results = evaluator.simple_evaluate(model="hf-causal-experimental", model_args=f"pretrained={model_id}", tasks=tasks, batch_size=8)
print(results)
```

Analysis/Plots
- Bar plots of PPL and zero-shot accuracies for each method at 2 and 3 bits.
- Throughput and memory comparisons (QELO must match PTQ/LoftQ within noise).

Experiment 3 — Component Ablations and Generalization
Goal
- Quantify the contribution of each QELO component and verify robustness across quantizers, bit-widths, LoRA ranks, and calibration sizes.

Ablations
- Components: {AWSVD, Learnable LUT, Error Shaping}. Evaluate all 2^3−1 non-empty subsets and Full.
- Quantizers: Uniform vs NF.
- Bits: 2-bit and 3-bit.
- LoRA rank: r in {4, 8, 16, 32}.
- Calibration size: N in {256, 512, 1024, 2048} sequences.

Procedure
1) Fix model: Pythia-1.4B.
2) Grid over the above factors (select a manageable subset via fractional factorial design, e.g., 24 runs total).
3) For each run, execute the QELO pipeline with components toggled.
4) Collect metrics:
   - WT2 perplexity; activation-weighted RMSE aggregated across layers; latent flip rate; offline time.
   - Convergence stability: fraction of layers whose loss decreased monotonically over outer iterations.
5) Report interactions: e.g., does Learnable LUT contribute more under Uniform or NF at 2-bit?

Success criteria
- Full QELO consistently best on activation-weighted RMSE and PPL across settings.
- AWSVD main effect: −5–10% RMSE vs standard SVD; Learnable LUT: −5–15%; Error shaping: −3–8%.
- Robustness: performance degrades gracefully with smaller calibration sizes; QELO still outperforms LoftQ.

Experiment 3: Python stubs
```python
from itertools import product

components = ["AWSVD","LUT","ERRSHAPE"]
quantizers = ["uniform","nf"]
bits_opts = [2,3]
ranks = [4,8,16]
calib_sizes = [512, 2048]

runs = []
for q, b, r, n in product(quantizers, bits_opts, ranks, calib_sizes):
    for mask in range(1, 2**len(components)):
        active = [components[i] for i in range(len(components)) if (mask>>i)&1]
        runs.append({"quantizer":q, "bits":b, "rank":r, "calib":n, "components":active})

def run_cfg(cfg):
    # build model, data loader of size cfg['calib']
    # run PTQ, then QELO with toggles
    # return dict of metrics
    pass

results = [run_cfg(cfg) for cfg in runs]
# aggregate and plot main effects and interactions
```

Notes on Implementation Details
- sqrt_block_diag/inv_sqrt_block_diag: implement by taking per-block eigendecomp (or diagonal only). For speed, use only diagonal of S.
- act_weighted_loss: compute with mini-batches of calibration activations X as ||Λ_out^{1/2} X E||_F^2 / ||Λ_out^{1/2} X W||_F^2.
- Bin-center regularization: encourage LUT centers toward empirical bin means of W within each code.
- EMA on LUT: apply after each optimizer step by moving parameters towards EMA buffer; or use Polyak averaging at evaluation time.
- Absorbing D and permutations: column permutations/signs remap LUT entries per block and reorder weight packing; diagonal row scales can be folded into next layer’s LayerNorm or output scale as in SmoothQuant.
- Safety checks: assert monotonicity of LUT centers; check no NaNs; clamp scales to reasonable ranges.

Potential Pitfalls and Mitigations
- Numerical instability in AWSVD with tiny Λ_out or S diagonal entries: add eps=1e-12; precondition by scaling.
- Overfitting LUT on small calibration: use EMA and early stopping; increase bin-center reg.
- Runtime drift after absorbing scales into LayerNorm: verify end-to-end outputs unchanged on a held-out small set.

Reproducibility
- Fix seeds for torch, numpy, and random.
- Log all hyperparameters and calibration sample IDs.
- Save per-layer metrics and artifacts (M, LUT, A,B) to allow exact replay.

Deliverables
- Plots and tables for Experiments 1–3.
- Scripts: qelo_calibrate.py, qelo_eval.py, qelo_ablate.py.
- A README with exact commands and expected wall-clock times.
experimental_design.experiment_details:
QELO — Activation-Weighted, Error-Shaping QLoRA: Detailed Experimental Plan and Code

Scope
- This specification operationalizes all items in the Experiment Strategy using PyTorch and HuggingFace. It integrates validation of activation-weighted objectives, stability and error shaping, end-to-end quality and cost, and component ablations/generalization, minimizing overlap by composing shared data/model tooling across experiments.
- Where possible, existing libraries are used: torch, transformers, datasets, lm-eval-harness, numpy/scipy (optional), wandb/tensorboard for logging. Minimal custom modules implement QELO-specific elements (AWSVD weighting, monotone LUT, signed permutations) while keeping quantization compatible with standard PTQ+PEFT flows.

Shared Implementation
1) Environment and Reproducibility
- Python ≥3.10, PyTorch ≥2.2 with CUDA 11.8+, transformers ≥4.42, datasets ≥2.19, tokenizers ≥0.15, lm-eval-harness ≥0.4.2, einops, numpy, wandb or tensorboard (optional), accelerate (optional for device map).
- Set seeds: torch.manual_seed, numpy.random.seed, random.seed. Determinism: torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(False) is acceptable; we log seeds and calibration indices.

2) Models and Data
- Models: EleutherAI/pythia-410m, EleutherAI/pythia-1.4b (optionally facebook/opt-1.3b). Load in float16 for base; quantize weights-only.
- Tokenizer: matching HF tokenizer.
- Calibration data: 512–2048 unlabeled sequences from wikitext-103-v1 train or C4 (subset). Sequence length=2048; pack without labels.
- Evaluation data: WikiText-2 (wt2) for perplexity; PTB (sanity ppl); lm-eval tasks: arc_easy, arc_challenge, piqa, hellaswag. For speed, cap each task to 1k examples (uniform sampling with fixed seed).

3) Quantization Configuration
- Weight quantization: per-output channel, grouped along input dimension, group size G ∈ {64, 128}. Bit-width b ∈ {2, 3}.
- Codebook scheme: uniform and NF-like (normal-float) initial codebooks. Integer codes M are frozen after initial PTQ; QELO only learns dequantization LUT θ and LoRA A,B and zero-overhead error shaping transforms.
- QELO defaults: LoRA rank r per layer=8 (3-bit) or 16 (2-bit); T=2 outer loops; 30 inner LUT/shape steps per loop; EMA β=0.95 on LUT; monotone LUT via cumulative softplus; bin-center regularizer λ=1e-3; signed permutations within blocks of G; diagonal scaling absorbed SmoothQuant-style.

4) Hooks to Capture Activations
- We use forward_pre_hook to capture each Linear’s input X (post-LN) and a forward hook for output Y, accumulating a sample buffer per layer until calibration budget is exhausted. Buffers are on GPU when possible; else CPU with fp16->fp32 conversion as needed.

5) Weighted Statistics
- S ≈ block-diagonal of X^T X / N per input group (G×G blocks). Default to diagonal-only approximation for speed; enable small banded option for sensitivity study.
- Λ_out ≈ var(Y) per output channel on calibration. Clamp with eps=1e-12 to ensure numerical stability.

6) Error-Shaping Absorption
- Column signed permutations are absorbed by reordering packed weight groups and remapping LUT indices; sign flips are absorbed by LUT code-to-center sign remapping. Row scales D_out are folded into following affine gains (LayerNorm/linear) akin to SmoothQuant; if not feasible, we maintain a folded per-row post-scale buffer and validate equivalence numerically on a tiny held-out set.

7) Logging and Reliability
- Per-layer metrics: activation-weighted RMSE, Frobenius error, latent flip rate, LUT delta norms, monotonicity violations (expect zero), time. End-to-end: ppl, zero-shot accuracies, tokens/s, memory.
- Cross-seed: seeds ∈ {0,1,2}. Confidence: report mean±std and paired relative improvements. Optional bootstrap over calibration subsets (k=5) for Experiment 1.

8) Minimal QELO Modules (shared across experiments)

Code: utilities for calibration and hooks
```
import torch, torch.nn as nn, torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
from datasets import load_dataset
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
from einops import rearrange
import math, random, time

def set_seeds(seed=0):
    import numpy as np, random
    torch.manual_seed(seed); torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(seed)
    np.random.seed(seed); random.seed(seed)

class ActBuffer:
    def __init__(self, max_elems=2_000_000, device='cuda'):
        self.max = max_elems; self.X = []; self.Y = []; self.n = 0; self.device = device
    def add(self, x, y):
        # x: [B*T, d_in], y: [B*T, d_out]
        with torch.no_grad():
            take = min(x.shape[0], max(0, self.max - self.n))
            if take <= 0: return
            self.X.append(x[:take].detach().to(self.device))
            self.Y.append(y[:take].detach().to(self.device))
            self.n += take
    def cat(self):
        return torch.cat(self.X, dim=0), torch.cat(self.Y, dim=0)

class LayerIOHook:
    def __init__(self, layer: nn.Linear, buf: ActBuffer):
        self.layer = layer; self.buf = buf
        self._x_cache = None
        self.h1 = layer.register_forward_pre_hook(self.pre_hook, with_kwargs=False)
        self.h2 = layer.register_forward_hook(self.post_hook)
    def pre_hook(self, module, inputs):
        x = inputs[0]
        self._x_cache = x.reshape(-1, x.shape[-1]).detach()
    def post_hook(self, module, inputs, output):
        y = output.reshape(-1, output.shape[-1]).detach()
        self.buf.add(self._x_cache, y)
        self._x_cache = None
    def remove(self):
        self.h1.remove(); self.h2.remove()

@torch.no_grad()
def estimate_S_Lambda(X, Y, group=64, diag_only=True):
    N, d_in = X.shape; d_out = Y.shape[1]
    nb = d_in // group
    blocks = []
    for b in range(nb):
        xb = X[:, b*group:(b+1)*group]
        Sb = (xb.t() @ xb) / max(1, xb.shape[0])
        if diag_only:
            Sb = torch.diag(torch.diag(Sb))
        blocks.append(Sb)
    S_blk = torch.block_diag(*blocks).to(X.device)
    Lambda_out = torch.var(Y, dim=0).clamp_min(1e-12)
    return S_blk, Lambda_out
```

Code: PTQ quantizers (uniform and NF-like) and LUTs
```
class UniformPTQ:
    def __init__(self, bits=3, group=64):
        self.bits = bits; self.group = group
        self.K = 2**bits
        # Symmetric signed: codes in [-(K/2), K/2 - 1]
        self.min_code = - (self.K // 2)
        self.max_code = (self.K // 2) - 1
    @torch.no_grad()
    def quantize(self, W):
        d_out, d_in = W.shape; G = self.group; nb = d_in // G
        M = torch.zeros_like(W, dtype=torch.int8)
        lut = []
        for b in range(nb):
            Wb = W[:, b*G:(b+1)*G]
            s = Wb.abs().amax(dim=1, keepdim=True) / max(1, self.max_code)
            Mb = torch.clamp((Wb / (s + 1e-12)).round(), self.min_code, self.max_code).to(torch.int8)
            # LUT centers are simply integer levels scaled by s per-row
            centers = torch.arange(self.min_code, self.max_code+1, device=W.device, dtype=W.dtype)
            lut.append({'type':'uniform', 'scale': s.squeeze(1), 'centers': centers})
            M[:, b*G:(b+1)*G] = Mb
        return M, lut

class NFLikePTQ:
    def __init__(self, bits=3, group=64):
        self.bits = bits; self.group = group; self.K = 2**bits
        # Build NF-like centers from normal quantiles, symmetric, unit max-abs
        k = self.K
        # Exclude infinities: use midpoints of k bins on (0,1)
        qs = (torch.arange(k, dtype=torch.float64) + 0.5) / k
        centers = torch.erfinv(2*qs-1) * math.sqrt(2)
        centers = centers - centers.mean()
        centers = (centers / centers.abs().max()).to(torch.float32)
        self.centers = centers  # [K]
    @torch.no_grad()
    def quantize(self, W):
        d_out, d_in = W.shape; G = self.group; nb = d_in // G
        M = torch.zeros_like(W, dtype=torch.int8)
        lut = []
        for b in range(nb):
            Wb = W[:, b*G:(b+1)*G]
            # per-row scale to match dynamic range
            s = (Wb.abs().amax(dim=1, keepdim=True) / self.centers.abs().max()).clamp_min(1e-8)
            # assign nearest center
            # broadcasting [d_out, G, 1] vs [K]
            dists = (Wb.unsqueeze(-1) / s - self.centers.view(1,1,-1)).abs()
            Mb = torch.argmin(dists, dim=-1).to(torch.int8)
            lut.append({'type':'nf', 'scale': s.squeeze(1), 'centers': self.centers.to(W.device)})
            M[:, b*G:(b+1)*G] = Mb
        return M, lut

class MonotoneLUT(nn.Module):
    # One learnable center vector per block; per-row scales remain external (γ in QELO can be folded into centers when uniform)
    def __init__(self, init_centers: torch.Tensor):
        super().__init__()
        init_centers = init_centers.detach().to(torch.float32)
        with torch.no_grad():
            diffs = torch.diff(init_centers)
            diffs = torch.clamp(diffs, min=1e-4)
            self.base = nn.Parameter(init_centers[:1].clone())
            self.logdiffs = nn.Parameter(torch.log(torch.expm1(diffs)))
    def centers(self):
        diffs_sp = F.softplus(self.logdiffs)
        return self.base + torch.cumsum(torch.cat([torch.zeros_like(self.base), diffs_sp], dim=0), dim=0)

@torch.no_grad()
def dequant_block(M_block: torch.Tensor, centers: torch.Tensor, scale_row: torch.Tensor=None):
    # M_block: [d_out, G] integer indices (for NF-like) or signed codes (for uniform remapped to indices)
    if M_block.dtype != torch.long:
        idx = M_block.long().clamp(0, centers.numel()-1)
    else:
        idx = M_block.clamp(0, centers.numel()-1)
    vals = centers[idx]
    if scale_row is not None:
        vals = vals * scale_row.unsqueeze(-1)
    return vals
```

Code: AWSVD and activation-weighted loss
```
def sqrt_block_diag(S_blk):
    # assumes block-diagonal or diagonal tensor
    return torch.linalg.cholesky(S_blk + torch.eye(S_blk.shape[0], device=S_blk.device)*1e-12) if S_blk.ndim==2 else torch.sqrt(S_blk)

def inv_sqrt_block_diag(S_blk):
    # numerically stable inverse sqrt for diagonal/blk-diag
    if torch.allclose(S_blk, torch.diag(torch.diag(S_blk))):
        d = torch.diag(S_blk).clamp_min(1e-12)
        return torch.diag(torch.rsqrt(d))
    # fallback via eig per block by slicing known group sizes in caller
    raise NotImplementedError('Provide blockwise inv sqrt in caller when using non-diagonal S_blk')

@torch.no_grad()
def aws_svd(R, S_blk, Lambda_out, rank=8, group=64):
    # Left weight: L^{1/2} = sqrt(Lambda_out), Right weight: S^{1/2}
    L_half = torch.sqrt(Lambda_out).clamp_min(1e-6)
    Rw = L_half[:, None] * R
    # Right multiply by S^{1/2} efficiently if S is block-diagonal-diagonal
    d_in = R.shape[1]; G = group
    nb = d_in // G
    cols = []
    for b in range(nb):
        Sb = S_blk[b*G:(b+1)*G, b*G:(b+1)*G]
        if torch.allclose(Sb, torch.diag(torch.diag(Sb))):
            cols.append(Rw[:, b*G:(b+1)*G] * torch.sqrt(torch.diag(Sb)).clamp_min(1e-6))
        else:
            cols.append(Rw[:, b*G:(b+1)*G] @ sqrt_block_diag(Sb))
    Rwr = torch.cat(cols, dim=1)
    # SVD
    U, Svals, Vt = torch.linalg.svd(Rwr, full_matrices=False)
    U = U[:, :rank]; V = Vt.t()[:, :rank]
    # Map back: A = U / L_half, B = S^{-1/2} @ V
    A = U / (L_half[:, None] + 1e-6)
    # Apply S^{-1/2}
    Bcols = []
    for b in range(nb):
        Sb = S_blk[b*G:(b+1)*G, b*G:(b+1)*G]
        Vb = V[b*G:(b+1)*G, :]
        if torch.allclose(Sb, torch.diag(torch.diag(Sb))):
            Bcols.append((Vb / torch.sqrt(torch.diag(Sb)).clamp_min(1e-6).unsqueeze(1)))
        else:
            # Solve S^{1/2} X = Vb => X = S^{-1/2} Vb
            Bcols.append(torch.linalg.solve(sqrt_block_diag(Sb), Vb))
    B = torch.cat(Bcols, dim=0)
    return A, B

def act_weighted_loss_from_samples(E, X, Lambda_out):
    # E: [d_out, d_in]; X: [N, d_in]
    # loss = || Λ_out^{1/2} X E ||_F^2 / ||Λ_out^{1/2} X W||_F^2 (the denominator is logged separately and cached)
    XE = X @ E.t()  # [N, d_out]
    L_half = torch.sqrt(Lambda_out).view(1, -1)
    num = torch.sum((XE * L_half)**2)
    return num
```

Code: Signed permutation heuristic and application
```
@torch.no_grad()
def signed_perm_block(Rb, Sb_diag, Lambda_out, max_swaps=None):
    # Rb: [d_out, G], Sb_diag: [G] (diagonal approx), Lambda_out: [d_out]
    # Sensitivity s_j ∝ sqrt(S_jj) * ||sqrt(Λ) * R[:,j]||_2
    Lh = torch.sqrt(Lambda_out).unsqueeze(1)
    col_norms = torch.linalg.vector_norm(Lh * Rb, dim=0)
    sens = torch.sqrt(Sb_diag).clamp_min(1e-6) * col_norms
    perm = torch.argsort(sens, descending=True)
    # sign to align with downstream sensitivity (optional; here match average sign under Λ)
    sign = torch.sign((Lh.squeeze(1) @ Rb)).sign()
    sign[sign==0] = 1
    return perm, sign

@torch.no_grad()
def apply_perm_sign_to_codes(M_block, perm, sign, centers):
    # Reorder columns and apply sign by remapping centers if uniform; for NF-like, incorporate sign via centers as well
    M_perm = M_block[:, perm]
    # sign absorption: we flip centers instead of codes for no runtime change
    new_centers = centers * sign.mean().sign() if centers.ndim==1 else centers
    return M_perm, new_centers
```

Code: Per-layer QELO optimization (skeleton)
```
class QELORunner:
    def __init__(self, bits=3, group=64, scheme='uniform', rank=8, ema=0.95, steps=30, T=2, lr=1e-2, bin_reg=1e-3):
        self.bits=bits; self.group=group; self.rank=rank; self.ema=ema; self.steps=steps; self.T=T; self.lr=lr; self.bin_reg=bin_reg
        self.scheme = scheme
        self.ptq = UniformPTQ(bits, group) if scheme=='uniform' else NFLikePTQ(bits, group)

    def init_luts(self, ptq_lut_list):
        lmods = []
        for entry in ptq_lut_list:
            centers = entry['centers']
            lmods.append(MonotoneLUT(centers.clone().to(torch.float32)))
        return nn.ModuleList(lmods)

    def dequant_all(self, M, lut_modules, scales_per_block):
        d_out, d_in = M.shape; G = self.group; nb = d_in // G
        parts = []
        for b in range(nb):
            Mb = M[:, b*G:(b+1)*G]
            centers = lut_modules[b].centers()
            sc = scales_per_block[b]  # [d_out]
            parts.append(dequant_block(Mb, centers, sc))
        return torch.cat(parts, dim=1)

    def bin_center_reg(self, W, M, lut_modules, scales):
        # Encourage centers toward empirical bin means (per block)
        reg = 0.0
        G = self.group; nb = M.shape[1] // G
        for b in range(nb):
            Mb = M[:, b*G:(b+1)*G]
            Wb = W[:, b*G:(b+1)*G]
            centers = lut_modules[b].centers()
            sc = scales[b].unsqueeze(1)
            # compute means per code index (approximate by treating integer codes as indices)
            K = centers.numel()
            for k in range(K):
                mask = (Mb == k)
                if mask.any():
                    target = (Wb[mask].mean() / (sc[mask.any(dim=1)].mean()+1e-6)).detach()
                    reg = reg + F.mse_loss(centers[k], target, reduction='sum')
        return self.bin_reg * reg

    def optimize_layer(self, W, X, Y):
        G=self.group
        # 1) stats
        S_blk, Lambda_out = estimate_S_Lambda(X, Y, group=G, diag_only=True)
        # 2) PTQ
        M, ptq_lut = self.ptq.quantize(W)
        # scales per block (per-row); for uniform, stored in ptq_lut; for NF-like, also per-row
        scales = [entry['scale'] for entry in ptq_lut]
        scales = [s.clone().to(W.device) for s in scales]
        # 3) Init learnable LUTs
        lut = self.init_luts(ptq_lut).to(W.device)
        # LoRA factors
        A = torch.zeros(W.shape[0], self.rank, device=W.device)
        B = torch.zeros(W.shape[1], self.rank, device=W.device)
        params = list(lut.parameters()) + [A, B]
        opt = torch.optim.Adam(params, lr=self.lr)
        # Cache denom for logging
        with torch.no_grad():
            denom = act_weighted_loss_from_samples(W*0 + W, X, Lambda_out).item()
        # EMA buffers (copy of parameters)
        ema_state = [p.detach().clone() for p in lut.parameters()]
        def ema_update():
            with torch.no_grad():
                for i, p in enumerate(lut.parameters()):
                    ema_state[i].mul_(self.ema).add_(p, alpha=1-self.ema)

        for t in range(self.T):
            # a) AWSVD on current residual
            with torch.no_grad():
                Wdq = self.dequant_all(M, lut, scales)
                R = W - Wdq
                A[:], B[:] = aws_svd(R, S_blk, Lambda_out, rank=self.rank, group=G)
            # b) Inner updates
            for s in range(self.steps):
                Wdq = self.dequant_all(M, lut, scales)
                E = W - Wdq - A @ B.t()
                loss_num = act_weighted_loss_from_samples(E, X, Lambda_out)
                reg = self.bin_center_reg(W, M, lut, scales)
                loss = loss_num / (denom + 1e-8) + reg
                opt.zero_grad(); loss.backward(); opt.step(); ema_update()
            # c) Error shaping per block
            with torch.no_grad():
                Wdq = self.dequant_all(M, lut, scales)
                Rcur = W - Wdq - A @ B.t()
                nb = W.shape[1] // G
                for b in range(nb):
                    Rb = Rcur[:, b*G:(b+1)*G]
                    Sb_diag = torch.diag(S_blk[b*G:(b+1)*G, b*G:(b+1)*G])
                    perm, sign = signed_perm_block(Rb, Sb_diag, Lambda_out)
                    # Apply: reorder columns and flip centers
                    Mb = M[:, b*G:(b+1)*G][:, perm]
                    M[:, b*G:(b+1)*G] = Mb
                    # Fold sign into centers
                    centers = lut[b].centers().data
                    lut[b].base.data = lut[b].base.data * sign.mean().sign()
        # Final artifacts
        return {
            'M': M, 'lut': lut, 'A': A, 'B': B,
            'scales': scales, 'S_blk': S_blk, 'Lambda_out': Lambda_out
        }
```

Experiment 1 — Layer-wise Microbenchmarks and Stability
Goal
- Validate that QELO targets the activation-weighted objective, stabilizes low-bit behavior (reduces latent code flips), and that signed permutations + diagonal scaling reduce error without inference overhead.

Design
- Model: Pythia-410M and 1.4B.
- Bits: 2 and 3. Group size: 64.
- Quantizers: uniform and NF-like.
- Methods: PTQ-only, LoftQ/QLoRA (Frobenius objective), AQLM-lite (activation-weighted AB only), QELO (full), and QELO variants (-AWSVD, -LUT, -ErrorShaping). All methods share the exact same initial PTQ codes M to ensure fairness.
- Calibration: 512–2048 sequences; seeds {0,1,2}.

Procedure
1) Collect activations via hooks for each Linear module until calibration budget is reached. Compute S_blk and Λ_out per layer.
2) Run initial PTQ to freeze codes M and initial LUT entries.
3) For each layer, run T outer loops:
   - AWSVD to update LoRA A,B using Λ_out and S weights.
   - LUT learning with monotone constraint, bin-center regularization, EMA.
   - Greedy error shaping per block: signed permutations using sensitivity heuristic; fold permutations/signs into LUT/packing; optionally fold row scales into following layer’s gains.
4) Record per-layer metrics before/after each step.

Metrics
- Activation-weighted RMSE ratio: ||Λ^{1/2} X E||_F / ||Λ^{1/2} X W||_F.
- Frobenius weight error ||E||_F (for comparison with LoftQ objective).
- Latent flip rate: fraction of elements whose argmin center would differ from the original code if re-quantized with current learned LUT (measured on W, not on activations).
- Error shaping delta: RMSE reduction attributable to permutations/scales when A,B,θ are fixed.
- Time per layer and wall-clock offline overhead.
- Stability: count monotonic decreases across outer loops; monotonicity violations in LUT.

Reliability
- Cross-seed mean±std; bootstrap over calibration minibatches (k=5) to estimate CI on RMSE improvements. Sanity-check exact functional equivalence before/after absorbing permutations/scales using a small held-out activation set.

Success Criteria
- QELO reduces activation-weighted RMSE 10–30% vs LoftQ/QLoRA at 2–3 bits across both models.
- ≥50% reduction in latent flip rate vs same-optimizer without monotone+EMA+bin-center.
- Signed permutations/scales alone provide ≥5% incremental RMSE reduction with no runtime overhead.

Code sketch to run Experiment 1 per layer
```
def run_layerbench(model_id='EleutherAI/pythia-410m', bits=2, group=64, scheme='uniform', rank=16, calib_batches=32, seed=0):
    set_seeds(seed)
    tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_id, torch_dtype=torch.float16, device_map='auto')
    # Data
    ds = load_dataset('wikitext', 'wikitext-103-v1', split='train')
    def collate(batch, seq_len=2048):
        txt = "\n\n".join([x['text'] for x in batch])
        ids = tok(txt, return_tensors='pt', truncation=True, max_length=seq_len).input_ids
        return ids.to(model.device)
    dl = DataLoader(ds.shuffle(seed=seed).select(range(2048)), batch_size=1, collate_fn=lambda b: {'input_ids': collate(b)})
    # Collect activations layer-wise
    lin_layers = [m for m in model.modules() if isinstance(m, nn.Linear)]
    layer_bufs = {id(l): ActBuffer(max_elems=300_000, device=model.device) for l in lin_layers}
    hooks = [LayerIOHook(l, layer_bufs[id(l)]) for l in lin_layers]
    with torch.no_grad():
        it = 0
        for batch in dl:
            _ = model(**batch)
            it += 1
            if it >= calib_batches: break
    for h in hooks: h.remove()

    results = []
    qelo = QELORunner(bits=bits, group=group, scheme=scheme, rank=rank, T=2, steps=30)
    for l in lin_layers:
        X, Y = layer_bufs[id(l)].cat()
        W = l.weight.detach().to(torch.float32)
        art = qelo.optimize_layer(W, X, Y)
        Wdq = qelo.dequant_all(art['M'], art['lut'], art['scales'])
        E = W - Wdq - art['A'] @ art['B'].t()
        num = act_weighted_loss_from_samples(E, X, art['Lambda_out']).item()
        denom = act_weighted_loss_from_samples(W*0 + W, X, art['Lambda_out']).item()
        rmse_ratio = math.sqrt(num/denom)
        results.append({'layer': str(l), 'rmse_ratio': rmse_ratio})
    return results
```

Analysis and Plots
- Plot RMSE ratio per layer across methods and averages with 95% CI across seeds. Histogram of latent flip rates. Ablate contributions of AWSVD, LUT, and error shaping within the same calibration.

Experiment 2 — End-to-End Language Modeling and Inference Cost Audit
Goal
- Demonstrate QELO improves perplexity and zero-shot accuracy at 2–3 bits without degrading inference throughput or memory vs PTQ-only/LoftQ.

Design
- Same models, bits, group sizes as Experiment 1. Methods: PTQ-only, LoftQ/QLoRA, AQLM-lite, QELO.
- For baselines, we implement:
  - LoftQ/QLoRA: identical outer loop but SVD on unweighted residual and AB trained to minimize ||W−Q−AB^T||_F using the same calibration samples.
  - AQLM-lite: activation-weighted AB only (no LUT learning nor permutations), matching calibration budget.
- QELO absorption ensures runtime kernels identical to PTQ+LoRA: forward computes Ŵ = dequant(M; θ*) + A*B*^T; no extra ops.

Tasks and Metrics
- WikiText-2 perplexity (val/test). PTB perplexity (sanity). lm-eval-harness tasks: arc_easy, arc_challenge, piqa, hellaswag (1k shots per task).
- Throughput: tokens/s for greedy generation with seq_len=2048, batch sizes {1, 8}. Latency p50/p95 on max_new_tokens=8. Peak memory (torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated()).

Procedure
1) Replace every Linear with a QuantizedLoRALinear module that holds frozen integer codes M, learnable LUT centers (frozen after QELO), and LoRA factors A,B; permutations/signs are already absorbed during packing.
2) Evaluate PPL and lm-eval tasks in fp16 computational graph.
3) Measure throughput before and after QELO; warm-up 20 steps; measure for 200 steps; synchronize CUDA.

Success Criteria
- QELO improves WT2 ppl by 5–15% vs LoftQ/QLoRA at 2-bit; 3–8% at 3-bit.
- +1–3 average points on zero-shot tasks at 2–3 bits.
- Throughput within ±1% and memory within noise vs PTQ-only/LoftQ.

Code: wrapping layers for runtime equivalence
```
class QuantizedLoRALinear(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, base: nn.Linear, M, lut_modules, scales, A, B, group=64):
        super().__init__()
        self.in_features = base.in_features; self.out_features = base.out_features; self.bias = base.bias
        self.register_buffer('M', M.to(torch.int8), persistent=False)
        self.lut = lut_modules  # ModuleList
        self.scales = scales
        self.A = nn.Parameter(A, requires_grad=False)
        self.B = nn.Parameter(B, requires_grad=False)
        self.group = group
    def dequant_weight(self):
        # reconstruct on-the-fly once and cache per eval epoch if memory permits
        with torch.no_grad():
            d_out, d_in = self.M.shape; G = self.group; nb = d_in // G
            parts = []
            for b in range(nb):
                Mb = self.M[:, b*G:(b+1)*G]
                centers = self.lut[b].centers()
                sc = self.scales[b]
                parts.append(dequant_block(Mb, centers, sc))
            Wdq = torch.cat(parts, dim=1)
        return Wdq
    def forward(self, x):
        W = self.dequant_weight() + self.A @ self.B.t()
        y = F.linear(x, W, self.bias)
        return y

def replace_with_qelo(model, layer_artifacts, group=64):
    i = 0
    for name, module in list(model.named_modules()):
        if isinstance(module, nn.Linear):
            art = layer_artifacts[i]; i += 1
            ql = QuantizedLoRALinear(module, art['M'], art['lut'], art['scales'], art['A'], art['B'], group)
            parent = model
            names = name.split('.')
            for n in names[:-1]: parent = getattr(parent, n)
            setattr(parent, names[-1], ql)
    return model
```

Code: PPL and throughput measurement
```
def eval_ppl(model, tok, dataset='wikitext2', split='test', seq_len=2048, batch_size=4):
    if dataset=='wikitext2':
        ds = load_dataset('wikitext', 'wikitext-2-raw-v1', split=split)
    elif dataset=='ptb':
        ds = load_dataset('ptb_text_only', 'penn_treebank', split=split)
    texts = ds['text']
    # pack into contiguous chunks
    ids = tok('\n'.join(texts), return_tensors='pt').input_ids.to(model.device)
    n = (ids.numel() // seq_len) * seq_len
    ids = ids[:, :n].reshape(1, -1)
    losses = []
    model.eval()
    with torch.no_grad():
        for i in range(0, ids.shape[1]-1, seq_len):
            x = ids[:, i:i+seq_len]
            out = model(input_ids=x, labels=x)
            losses.append(out.loss.float().item())
    import math
    ppl = math.exp(sum(losses)/len(losses))
    return ppl

def measure_tput(model, tok, prompt='Hello world', warmup=20, steps=200):
    input_ids = tok(prompt, return_tensors='pt').input_ids.to(model.device)
    # warmup
    for _ in range(warmup):
        _ = model.generate(input_ids, max_new_tokens=8)
    torch.cuda.synchronize(); t0=time.time(); n_toks=0
    for _ in range(steps):
        out = model.generate(input_ids, max_new_tokens=8)
```
